meta.certificationName: Databricks Certified Associate Developer for Apache Spark
meta.title: DBX-SDA-1: Spark Architecture, RDDs and DataFrames

questions.stem: In a Spark cluster, which component is responsible for running the `main()` function of a Spark application and creating the `SparkSession`?

options.text: Executor
Driver
Cluster Manager
Worker Node

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct: the Driver runs the main() function, creates the SparkSession/SparkContext, builds the DAG, schedules tasks, and collects results.

**A** is wrong: Executors run individual tasks on partitions and cache data, but do not run the main application logic.

**C** is wrong: the Cluster Manager (YARN, Mesos, Kubernetes, Standalone) allocates resources but does not run application code.

**D** is wrong: Worker Nodes host Executors; they do not run the main() function directly.

[Spark Cluster Mode Overview](https://spark.apache.org/docs/latest/cluster-overview.html)

questions.stem: A Spark job performs the following sequence of operations on a DataFrame: `filter()` → `select()` → `groupBy()` → `agg()` → `filter()` → `count()`. How many stages will this job have at minimum?
questions.explanation:
**C** is correct: stage boundaries are created at shuffle points. The `groupBy().agg()` introduces a shuffle (wide transformation), creating 2 stages: Stage 1 covers filter→select→groupBy shuffle write, Stage 2 covers shuffle read→agg→filter→count.

**A** is wrong: at least one shuffle creates at least 2 stages.

**B** is wrong: there is only one shuffle point (groupBy), not two, so only 2 stages are produced.

**D** is wrong: the number of stages is determined by shuffles, not by the total number of transformation steps.

[Spark Cluster Mode Overview](https://spark.apache.org/docs/latest/cluster-overview.html)

questions.stem: Which of the following is a **wide transformation** in Spark?

options.text: `df.filter(col('age') > 18)`
`df.select('name', 'age')`
`df.map(lambda x: x.upper())`
`df.distinct()`

questions.explanation:
**D** is correct: `distinct()` requires comparing records across all partitions to remove duplicates, which requires a shuffle — making it a wide transformation.

**A** is wrong: `filter()` is a narrow transformation; each output partition depends on exactly one input partition, requiring no shuffle.

**B** is wrong: `select()` is a narrow transformation that operates on each partition independently.

**C** is wrong: `map()` is a narrow transformation applied element-by-element within each partition.

[Spark Cluster Mode Overview](https://spark.apache.org/docs/latest/cluster-overview.html)

questions.stem: You have a Spark application processing 200 GB of data. After a `groupBy().agg()` step, your job is slow and Spark UI shows most tasks completing quickly but a few taking 10x longer. The default `spark.sql.shuffle.partitions` is set to 200. What is the most likely cause and fix?

options.text: The number of executors is too low; increase executor count.
The driver is out of memory; increase `spark.driver.memory`.
Lazy evaluation is causing recomputation; call `df.cache()` before the groupBy.
Data skew — some keys appear far more frequently, causing uneven partition sizes after shuffle. Increase `spark.sql.shuffle.partitions` to 2000.

questions.explanation:
**D** is correct: when a few tasks run 10x longer than others after a shuffle, this is the classic symptom of data skew — high-frequency keys land in a single partition, creating a large task while others finish quickly. Increasing shuffle partitions spreads work more finely, though salt-key techniques are the deeper fix.

**A** is wrong: adding executors does not help if one key's data all maps to one partition; the slow task still runs on one core.

**B** is wrong: the OOM would be on the executor, not the driver, and the symptom would be task failures rather than slowness.

**C** is wrong: caching before the groupBy can help avoid re-reading data but does not address the uneven distribution after the shuffle.

[Spark SQL Performance Tuning](https://spark.apache.org/docs/latest/sql-performance-tuning.html)

questions.stem: Select TWO statements that are TRUE about Spark transformations and actions. (Select TWO)

options.text: Transformations are lazy — they build a DAG but do not execute until an action is called.
`df.count()` is a transformation that returns a new DataFrame with one row.
`df.show()` triggers the execution of all preceding transformations.
`df.filter()` immediately reads data from disk and filters it partition by partition.

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct: Spark uses lazy evaluation — transformations like filter, select, and join define the computation graph (DAG) but no data is moved or computed until an action forces execution.

**C** is correct: `show()` is an action that triggers computation of all upstream transformations and displays the first N rows.

**B** is wrong: `count()` is an action that returns a Python/Scala Long, not a new DataFrame.

**D** is wrong: `filter()` is a lazy transformation; no data is read or filtered at the moment it is called.

[Spark Cluster Mode Overview](https://spark.apache.org/docs/latest/cluster-overview.html)

questions.stem: Which of the following correctly reads a CSV file with a header row and infers the schema automatically?

options.text: `spark.read.csv('/path/to/file.csv')`
`spark.read.format('csv').option('header', 'true').load('/path/to/file.csv')`
`spark.read.format('csv').option('header', 'true').option('inferSchema', 'true').load('/path/to/file.csv')`
`spark.read.csv('/path/to/file.csv', header=True)`

questions.explanation:
**C** is correct: both `header='true'` and `inferSchema='true'` options must be set to use column names from the first row and automatically detect data types.

**A** is wrong: without options, the CSV reader does not treat the first row as a header and reads all columns as strings.

**B** is wrong: `header='true'` uses column names but without `inferSchema='true'` all columns are still read as StringType.

**D** is wrong: while the shorthand `spark.read.csv()` accepts keyword arguments, `inferSchema=True` is also required but missing here.

[Spark SQL, DataFrames and Datasets Guide](https://spark.apache.org/docs/latest/sql-programming-guide.html)

questions.stem: Given `df` with columns `['id', 'name', 'age', 'salary']`, which expression adds a new column `senior` that is `True` when `age >= 60`?

options.text: `df.select('senior', col('age') >= 60)`
`df.addColumn('senior', col('age') >= 60)`
`df.withColumn('senior', col('age') >= 60)`
`df.withColumn('senior', 'age >= 60')`

questions.explanation:
**C** is correct: `withColumn(name, column_expr)` adds or replaces a column using a Column expression; `col('age') >= 60` produces a BooleanType Column.

**A** is wrong: `select()` returns only the listed columns — using `'senior'` as the first argument would try to select an existing column named 'senior', and the syntax is incorrect for adding a derived column.

**B** is wrong: `addColumn()` is not a valid DataFrame method in PySpark.

**D** is wrong: passing a plain string `'age >= 60'` is not interpreted as an expression; it would raise an AnalysisException because no column named 'age >= 60' exists.

[Spark SQL, DataFrames and Datasets Guide](https://spark.apache.org/docs/latest/sql-programming-guide.html)

questions.stem: You need to rename column `emp_id` to `employee_id` in a DataFrame. Which of the following is the correct approach?

options.text: `df.select(col('emp_id').name('employee_id'))`
`df.withColumn('employee_id', col('emp_id')).drop('employee_id')`
`df.rename('emp_id', 'employee_id')`
`df.withColumnRenamed('emp_id', 'employee_id')`

questions.explanation:
**D** is correct: `withColumnRenamed(existingName, newName)` is the standard PySpark method to rename a column.

**C** is wrong: `rename()` is not a method on PySpark DataFrames (it exists on Pandas DataFrames).

**B** is wrong: this drops 'employee_id' (the new column) instead of 'emp_id' (the old one), effectively doing nothing useful.

**A** is wrong: `.name()` is not a valid Column method in PySpark; the correct alias method is `.alias()`.

[Spark SQL, DataFrames and Datasets Guide](https://spark.apache.org/docs/latest/sql-programming-guide.html)

questions.stem:
What does the following PySpark code return?

```python
from pyspark.sql.functions import col
df.filter((col('status') == 'active') & (col('score') > 80) | (col('vip') == True))
```

options.text: Rows where status is 'active' AND (score > 80 OR vip is True)
Rows where status is 'active' AND score > 80 AND vip is True
A syntax error because Python bitwise operators cannot be used in Spark filter conditions
Rows where (status is 'active' AND score > 80) OR vip is True

questions.explanation:
**D** is correct: Python's bitwise AND (`&`) has higher precedence than bitwise OR (`|`), so the expression is parsed as `(col('status') == 'active') & (col('score') > 80)` evaluated first, then OR'd with `col('vip') == True`. This matches rows where (status='active' AND score>80) OR vip=True.

**A** is wrong: that would require explicit parentheses around `(col('score') > 80) | (col('vip') == True)`.

**B** is wrong: this describes AND of all three conditions.

**C** is wrong: bitwise operators `&`, `|`, and `~` are the correct Spark-safe operators for Column logical expressions; Python's `and`/`or`/`not` do not work correctly with Column objects.

[Spark SQL, DataFrames and Datasets Guide](https://spark.apache.org/docs/latest/sql-programming-guide.html)

questions.stem: You want to find the top 3 highest-paid employees in each department. Which code correctly achieves this using window functions?

options.text:
```python
df.groupBy('dept').agg(max('salary').alias('salary')).limit(3)
```

```python
from pyspark.sql.window import Window
from pyspark.sql.functions import row_number
w = Window.orderBy(col('salary').desc())
df.withColumn('rnk', row_number().over(w)).filter(col('rnk') <= 3)
```

```python
from pyspark.sql.window import Window
from pyspark.sql.functions import rank
w = Window.partitionBy('dept').orderBy(col('salary').desc())
df.withColumn('rnk', rank().over(w)).filter(col('rnk') <= 3)
```

```python
df.sort(col('salary').desc()).groupBy('dept').head(3)
```

questions.explanation:
**C** is correct: `Window.partitionBy('dept').orderBy(col('salary').desc())` computes rank within each department; filtering `rnk <= 3` keeps top-3 per department. Note: `rank()` skips numbers on ties (1,1,3); `dense_rank()` would also be acceptable.

**A** is wrong: this finds the single maximum salary per department, not the top 3 employees.

**B** is wrong: the window lacks `partitionBy('dept')`, so `row_number()` is computed globally across all rows — not per department.

**D** is wrong: `groupBy().head()` is not valid PySpark syntax; `head()` on a DataFrame returns rows to the driver, and chaining it after `groupBy()` is not a valid operation.

[Spark SQL, DataFrames and Datasets Guide](https://spark.apache.org/docs/latest/sql-programming-guide.html)

questions.stem: You have two DataFrames: `orders` (10 million rows) and `products` (500 rows). You join them on `product_id`. The join is running slowly due to a SortMergeJoin. Which change will most likely convert this to a BroadcastHashJoin and improve performance?

options.text:
```python
from pyspark.sql.functions import broadcast
orders.join(broadcast(products), 'product_id')
```
`spark.conf.set('spark.sql.shuffle.partitions', '10')`
`orders.join(products, 'product_id').hint('sort_merge')`
`orders.repartition(500, 'product_id').join(products, 'product_id')`

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct: wrapping the smaller DataFrame with `broadcast()` hints to Spark to broadcast `products` to all executor nodes, eliminating the shuffle entirely and converting the join to a BroadcastHashJoin. This is the canonical approach when one side is small enough to fit in memory.

**C** is wrong: `hint('sort_merge')` forces a SortMergeJoin, which is the opposite of what we want.

**B** is wrong: reducing shuffle partitions changes partition count but does not change the join strategy from SortMergeJoin to BroadcastHashJoin.

**D** is wrong: repartitioning on the join key can speed up SortMergeJoin slightly by pre-shuffling, but it does not convert the join to a BroadcastHashJoin.

[Spark SQL Performance Tuning](https://spark.apache.org/docs/latest/sql-performance-tuning.html)

questions.stem: What is the key difference between `repartition(n)` and `coalesce(n)` when reducing the number of partitions?

options.text: `repartition()` can only increase partitions; `coalesce()` can only decrease partitions.
`coalesce()` performs a full shuffle for optimal data distribution; `repartition()` merges partitions without a shuffle.
Both operations perform a shuffle, but `coalesce()` is faster because it uses a hash-based distribution.
`repartition()` performs a full shuffle and can increase or decrease partitions; `coalesce()` avoids a shuffle by merging existing partitions and can only decrease partitions.

questions.explanation:
**D** is correct: `repartition(n)` does a full shuffle to redistribute data evenly across exactly n partitions, and can both increase and decrease the count. `coalesce(n)` avoids a shuffle by combining adjacent partitions on the same executor, but can only reduce partition count (not increase it) and may result in uneven partition sizes.

**A** is wrong: `repartition()` can both increase and decrease partitions (not only increase).

**B** is wrong: this reverses the behaviors — `coalesce()` avoids shuffle, `repartition()` causes shuffle.

**C** is wrong: `coalesce()` does NOT perform a full shuffle; avoiding the shuffle is its primary advantage.

[Spark RDD Programming Guide](https://spark.apache.org/docs/latest/rdd-programming-guide.html)

questions.stem: You need to perform a left anti join to find all customers in `customers` who have NOT placed any orders in `orders`. Which code is correct?

options.text: `customers.subtract(orders)`
`customers.join(orders, 'customer_id', how='left')`
`customers.join(orders, 'customer_id', how='left_semi')`
`customers.join(orders, 'customer_id', how='left_anti')`

questions.explanation:
**D** is correct: a left anti join returns only rows from the left DataFrame (`customers`) that have NO matching rows in the right DataFrame (`orders`). This is exactly the 'customers without orders' use case.

**B** is wrong: a left join returns ALL rows from `customers`, including those with orders, with NULLs for non-matching `orders` columns; you would need an extra filter for `orders.customer_id IS NULL`.

**C** is wrong: a left semi join is the opposite — it returns rows from `customers` that DO have a matching row in `orders`.

**A** is wrong: `subtract()` works on DataFrames with identical schemas and subtracts rows, but `customers` and `orders` likely have different schemas.

[Spark SQL, DataFrames and Datasets Guide](https://spark.apache.org/docs/latest/sql-programming-guide.html)

questions.stem:
You define a UDF as follows:

```python
from pyspark.sql.functions import udf
from pyspark.sql.types import IntegerType

def double_it(x):
    return x * 2

double_udf = udf(double_it, IntegerType())
result = df.withColumn('doubled', double_udf(col('value')))
```

What is the main performance concern with this approach compared to using built-in Spark functions?

options.text: Python UDFs require serializing each row from the JVM to a Python process and back, bypassing Spark's Catalyst optimizer — leading to high serialization overhead.
The `IntegerType()` return annotation is unnecessary and causes a schema mismatch error.
UDFs cannot accept Column objects as arguments; they require Python literals.
UDFs are faster than built-in functions because Python is closer to the data layer.

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct: Python UDFs break out of the JVM execution path — Spark must serialize each row to a Python process via Py4J/pickle, execute the Python function, then deserialize the result back. This per-row serialization overhead is the main performance cost, and Catalyst cannot optimize inside the UDF.

**C** is wrong: UDFs accept Column expressions as arguments (as shown in the code).

**B** is wrong: the return type annotation is required, not unnecessary — without it Spark defaults to StringType.

**D** is wrong: built-in Spark functions run natively in the JVM and are optimized by Catalyst, making them significantly faster than Python UDFs.

[User-Defined Functions (UDFs) — Databricks](https://docs.databricks.com/en/udf/index.html)

questions.stem: Select TWO methods that can be used to handle null values in a PySpark DataFrame. (Select TWO)

options.text: `df.fillna({'age': 0, 'name': 'unknown'})`
`df.dropna(thresh=2, subset=['age', 'salary'])`
`df.removeNulls(cols=['age', 'salary'])`
`df.null.fill(0)`

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct: `fillna()` (also `na.fill()`) replaces null values — passing a dict replaces nulls in specified columns with their mapped values.

**B** is correct: `dropna(thresh=2, subset=['age', 'salary'])` drops rows where fewer than 2 non-null values exist among the specified columns.

**C** is wrong: `removeNulls()` is not a valid PySpark DataFrame method.

**D** is wrong: `df.null` is not a valid attribute on PySpark DataFrames; the correct accessor for null-handling methods is `df.na` (e.g., `df.na.fill(0)`).

[Spark SQL, DataFrames and Datasets Guide](https://spark.apache.org/docs/latest/sql-programming-guide.html)

questions.stem: Which function correctly extracts the year from a date column named `event_date`?

options.text: `df.withColumn('yr', F.date_part('year', col('event_date')))`
`df.withColumn('yr', col('event_date').year)`
`df.withColumn('yr', F.year(col('event_date')))`
`df.withColumn('yr', F.extract('year', col('event_date')))`

questions.explanation:
**C** is correct: `F.year(column)` is the standard PySpark function that extracts the year as an integer from a DateType or TimestampType column.

**A** is wrong: `date_part()` exists in Spark SQL syntax but `F.date_part()` is not a standard function in the pyspark.sql.functions module in all versions.

**B** is wrong: PySpark Column objects do not have a `.year` attribute; this is a Pandas API pattern.

**D** is wrong: `F.extract()` is not a function in pyspark.sql.functions; SQL's EXTRACT is available via `spark.sql()` or `expr()`, but not as `F.extract()`.

[Spark SQL, DataFrames and Datasets Guide](https://spark.apache.org/docs/latest/sql-programming-guide.html)

questions.stem:
You have a DataFrame with a column `tags` of type `ArrayType(StringType())`. You want to explode the array so each tag becomes its own row. What does the following code produce?

```python
from pyspark.sql.functions import explode
result = df.select('id', explode('tags').alias('tag'))
```

options.text: A DataFrame with one row per original row, where the `tags` column is replaced with the first element.
A DataFrame where rows with null or empty `tags` arrays are kept with a null `tag` value.
An error because `explode()` requires the column to be passed as a Column object, not a string.
A DataFrame with one row per element in the `tags` array, with `id` repeated for each tag.

questions.explanation:
**D** is correct: `explode()` generates one row per element in the array. If a row has `id=1` and `tags=['spark','python','sql']`, the result has three rows: (1,'spark'), (1,'python'), (1,'sql').

**A** is wrong: `explode()` generates multiple rows, not a single row per original row.

**B** is wrong: this describes `explode_outer()` behavior — `explode()` drops rows where the array is null or empty.

**C** is wrong: `explode()` accepts both a string column name and a Column object.

[Spark SQL, DataFrames and Datasets Guide](https://spark.apache.org/docs/latest/sql-programming-guide.html)

questions.stem: Which write mode will raise an error if the output path already exists?

options.text: `df.write.mode('overwrite').parquet('/output/path')`
`df.write.mode('append').parquet('/output/path')`
`df.write.mode('ignore').parquet('/output/path')`
`df.write.mode('error').parquet('/output/path')`

questions.explanation:
**D** is correct: `mode('error')` (also the default behavior when no mode is specified, equivalent to `mode('errorifexists')`) throws an AnalysisException if the target path already exists.

**A** is wrong: `mode('overwrite')` deletes the existing data and writes new data.

**B** is wrong: `mode('append')` adds new data alongside existing data.

**C** is wrong: `mode('ignore')` does nothing if the path already exists — it silently skips the write.

[Spark SQL, DataFrames and Datasets Guide](https://spark.apache.org/docs/latest/sql-programming-guide.html)

questions.stem:
You are computing a running total of sales per region ordered by date. Select TWO options that correctly implement this using window functions.

```python
from pyspark.sql.window import Window
from pyspark.sql.functions import sum as spark_sum, col
``` (Select TWO)

options.text:
```python
w = Window.partitionBy('region').orderBy('sale_date')
df.withColumn('running_total', spark_sum('amount').over(w))
```

```python
w = Window.partitionBy('region').orderBy('sale_date').rowsBetween(Window.unboundedPreceding, Window.currentRow)
df.withColumn('running_total', spark_sum('amount').over(w))
```

```python
w = Window.partitionBy('region').orderBy('sale_date').rangeBetween(Window.unboundedPreceding, Window.unboundedFollowing)
df.withColumn('running_total', spark_sum('amount').over(w))
```

```python
w = Window.orderBy('sale_date')
df.withColumn('running_total', spark_sum('amount').over(w))
```

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct: when `orderBy` is specified without an explicit frame, Spark defaults to `rangeBetween(unboundedPreceding, currentRow)` for aggregate functions, which computes a running total up to and including the current row.

**B** is correct: explicitly setting `rowsBetween(Window.unboundedPreceding, Window.currentRow)` achieves the same running total — summing from the first row in the partition up to the current physical row.

**C** is wrong: `rangeBetween(unboundedPreceding, unboundedFollowing)` sums ALL rows in the partition, producing the partition total for every row, not a running total.

**D** is wrong: no `partitionBy()` means the window spans ALL rows globally, not per-region — the running total would cross region boundaries.

[Spark SQL, DataFrames and Datasets Guide](https://spark.apache.org/docs/latest/sql-programming-guide.html)

questions.stem: You cache a DataFrame using `df.cache()` and then run two separate actions on it. Later you want to free the cached data. Which call correctly removes it from the cache?

options.text: `df.cache().clear()`
`df.uncache()`
`df.unpersist()`
`spark.catalog.clearCache()` — this is the only way to remove a single DataFrame from cache.

questions.explanation:
**C** is correct: `df.unpersist()` removes the specific DataFrame from the Spark cache and frees the storage memory. It is the correct counterpart to `df.cache()` or `df.persist()`.

**A** is wrong: `df.cache().clear()` is not valid — `cache()` returns the DataFrame itself, and DataFrames do not have a `.clear()` method.

**B** is wrong: `uncache()` is not a valid PySpark DataFrame method; the correct method is `unpersist()`.

**D** is wrong: `spark.catalog.clearCache()` removes ALL cached tables/DataFrames from the cache, not just a single one — it is not the most targeted approach.

[Spark SQL, DataFrames and Datasets Guide](https://spark.apache.org/docs/latest/sql-programming-guide.html)

questions.stem:
Given the following code, what does `collect_set` return compared to `collect_list`?

```python
df.groupBy('dept').agg(
    F.collect_list('skill').alias('all_skills'),
    F.collect_set('skill').alias('unique_skills')
)
```

options.text: `collect_list` sorts the values; `collect_set` does not guarantee order.
`collect_set` collects values into a Python set type; `collect_list` collects values into a Python list type.
`collect_list` collects all values including duplicates; `collect_set` collects only unique values and returns them in an unspecified order.
Both return the same result, but `collect_set` is faster because it avoids sorting.

questions.explanation:
**C** is correct: `collect_list()` gathers all values (including duplicates) for each group into an ArrayType column; `collect_set()` gathers only distinct values into an ArrayType column with no guaranteed ordering.

**A** is wrong: neither `collect_list` nor `collect_set` guarantees sorted output.

**B** is wrong: both return ArrayType (Spark array) columns, not Python set/list types — the column type in the DataFrame schema is ArrayType for both.

**D** is wrong: they do not return the same result because `collect_set` deduplicates values.

[Spark SQL, DataFrames and Datasets Guide](https://spark.apache.org/docs/latest/sql-programming-guide.html)

questions.stem: Select TWO string functions from `pyspark.sql.functions` that can be used to clean and transform text data. (Select TWO)

options.text: `F.regexp_replace(col('text'), '[^a-zA-Z]', '')`
`F.split(col('full_name'), ' ')`
`F.str_replace(col('text'), 'old', 'new')`
`F.string_split(col('full_name'), ',')`

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct: `F.regexp_replace(column, pattern, replacement)` replaces all substrings matching a regex pattern — useful for cleaning non-alphabetic characters.

**B** is correct: `F.split(column, pattern)` splits a string by a delimiter or regex pattern, returning an ArrayType column — useful for parsing names or CSV-in-a-column.

**C** is wrong: `F.str_replace()` is not a function in pyspark.sql.functions; the correct function is `F.regexp_replace()`.

**D** is wrong: `F.string_split()` is not a valid pyspark.sql.functions function; the correct name is `F.split()`.

[Spark SQL, DataFrames and Datasets Guide](https://spark.apache.org/docs/latest/sql-programming-guide.html)

questions.stem: Before you can query a DataFrame using `spark.sql('SELECT * FROM my_table')`, what must you do?

options.text: Write the DataFrame to a Delta table first.
Call `df.registerSQL('my_table')` to expose it to the SQL engine.
Nothing; Spark automatically exposes all DataFrames to the SQL engine by their variable name.
Register the DataFrame as a temporary view using `df.createOrReplaceTempView('my_table')`.

questions.explanation:
**D** is correct: `createOrReplaceTempView('name')` registers the DataFrame as a temporary view in the Spark catalog for the current SparkSession, after which you can query it by that name using `spark.sql()`.

**A** is wrong: while writing to a Delta table also makes it queryable, it is not required — temp views are lightweight and do not persist data to disk.

**B** is wrong: `registerSQL()` is not a valid method on PySpark DataFrames.

**C** is wrong: Spark does not automatically expose DataFrames by their Python variable names; explicit registration is required.

[Spark SQL, DataFrames and Datasets Guide](https://spark.apache.org/docs/latest/sql-programming-guide.html)

questions.stem: What SQL window function syntax correctly computes a running total of `amount` per `customer_id`, ordered by `order_date`?

options.text: `SUM(amount) OVER (ORDER BY order_date PARTITION BY customer_id)`
`SUM(amount) GROUP BY customer_id ORDER BY order_date`
`RUNNING_SUM(amount) OVER (PARTITION BY customer_id ORDER BY order_date)`
`SUM(amount) OVER (PARTITION BY customer_id ORDER BY order_date)`

questions.explanation:
**D** is correct: the SQL window function syntax uses `AGGREGATE OVER (PARTITION BY ... ORDER BY ...)`. With `ORDER BY` in the frame, Spark defaults to a cumulative frame (RANGE BETWEEN UNBOUNDED PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW), producing a running total per customer.

**B** is wrong: `GROUP BY` with `SUM` computes a total per group and collapses rows — it is not a window function and does not produce a running total.

**C** is wrong: `RUNNING_SUM()` is not a valid SQL function in Spark SQL.

**A** is wrong: the `OVER` clause syntax requires `PARTITION BY` before `ORDER BY` — reversing them causes a SQL parse error.

[Spark SQL, DataFrames and Datasets Guide](https://spark.apache.org/docs/latest/sql-programming-guide.html)

questions.stem: Which Spark SQL statement creates a view that can be queried only within the current SparkSession?

options.text: `CREATE TABLE my_view AS SELECT * FROM source_table`
`CREATE VIEW my_view AS SELECT * FROM source_table`
`CREATE OR REPLACE TEMP VIEW my_view AS SELECT * FROM source_table`
`CREATE GLOBAL TEMP VIEW my_view AS SELECT * FROM source_table`

questions.explanation:
**C** is correct: `CREATE OR REPLACE TEMP VIEW` creates a view that is scoped to the current SparkSession; it is dropped automatically when the session ends and is not visible to other sessions.

**A** is wrong: `CREATE TABLE ... AS SELECT` creates a persistent table, not a session-scoped view.

**B** is wrong: `CREATE VIEW` (without TEMP) creates a persistent view in the metastore, visible across sessions.

**D** is wrong: `CREATE GLOBAL TEMP VIEW` creates a view in the `global_temp` database that persists across sessions within the same Spark application (but is still temporary); it is accessible to other sessions using `global_temp.my_view`.

[Unity Catalog — Create Metastore](https://docs.databricks.com/data-governance/unity-catalog/create-metastore.html)

questions.stem: You run `spark.catalog.tableExists('sales')` after creating a temp view with `df.createOrReplaceTempView('sales')`. What does this return, and what does `spark.catalog.listTables()` show?

options.text: `tableExists()` returns `True`; `listTables()` shows `sales` with `tableType = 'TEMPORARY'`.
`tableExists()` returns `True`; `listTables()` shows `sales` with `tableType = 'MANAGED'`.
`tableExists()` returns `False` because temp views are not tables; `listTables()` shows no entries.
`tableExists()` raises a NameError because temporary views must be checked with `spark.catalog.viewExists()`.

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct: the Spark catalog treats temporary views as queryable objects; `tableExists('sales')` returns `True` for both temp views and permanent tables. `listTables()` returns them with `tableType='TEMPORARY'` to distinguish them from MANAGED or EXTERNAL tables.

**C** is wrong: temp views ARE visible in the catalog; `tableExists()` returns True.

**B** is wrong: `MANAGED` is a tableType for tables stored in the Spark warehouse, not for temp views.

**D** is wrong: there is no `viewExists()` method on the catalog; `tableExists()` covers both tables and views.

[Unity Catalog — Create Tables](https://docs.databricks.com/data-governance/unity-catalog/create-tables.html)

questions.stem: Select TWO Spark SQL statements that are valid for inspecting metadata about a table named `employees`. (Select TWO)

options.text: `DESCRIBE TABLE employees`
`SHOW SCHEMA employees`
`SHOW COLUMNS IN employees`
`INSPECT TABLE employees`

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct: `DESCRIBE TABLE table_name` (or just `DESCRIBE table_name`) returns the column names, data types, and comments for the specified table.

**C** is correct: `SHOW COLUMNS IN table_name` returns a list of column names for the specified table.

**B** is wrong: `SHOW SCHEMA` is not valid Spark SQL syntax; the correct command is `SHOW DATABASES` or `DESCRIBE DATABASE`.

**D** is wrong: `INSPECT TABLE` is not a valid Spark SQL command.

[Spark SQL, DataFrames and Datasets Guide](https://spark.apache.org/docs/latest/sql-programming-guide.html)

questions.stem: You call `df.explain()` on a joined DataFrame and see `BroadcastHashJoin` in the physical plan. What does this indicate?

options.text: Spark could not complete the join and fell back to a Python implementation.
The join required sorting both sides before joining, causing a full shuffle.
The join was performed on the driver, which collected both DataFrames before merging.
One side of the join was small enough (below `autoBroadcastJoinThreshold`) to be broadcast to all executors, avoiding a shuffle.

questions.explanation:
**D** is correct: `BroadcastHashJoin` means one side of the join was broadcast to all executors as a hash table, eliminating the need for a shuffle on that side. This is the most efficient join strategy when one table is small.

**A** is wrong: `BroadcastHashJoin` is a JVM-native, highly optimized Spark join strategy, not a Python fallback.

**B** is wrong: this describes `SortMergeJoin`, which sorts both sides and requires a shuffle exchange on both sides.

**C** is wrong: joins are performed distributed across executors, not on the driver.

[Spark SQL Performance Tuning — Join Strategies](https://spark.apache.org/docs/latest/sql-performance-tuning.html)

questions.stem: Your PySpark job fails with `AnalysisException: Resolved attribute missing from child`. What is the most likely cause?

options.text: A column referenced in a transformation does not exist in the DataFrame at that point in the plan.
The SparkSession was not initialized before running the job.
An executor ran out of memory while processing a large partition.
A shuffle produced more data than `spark.sql.shuffle.partitions` can handle.

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct: `AnalysisException` occurs during the analysis phase (before execution) when Spark cannot resolve a column name against the DataFrame schema. Common causes include a misspelled column name, referencing a column that was dropped, or joining two DataFrames with ambiguous columns.

**C** is wrong: executor OOM produces a `java.lang.OutOfMemoryError` at runtime, not an AnalysisException at planning time.

**B** is wrong: an uninitialized SparkSession would raise an AttributeError in Python before any Spark operations.

**D** is wrong: too many shuffle partitions would cause memory pressure or slowness, not an AnalysisException.

[Spark SQL Performance Tuning](https://spark.apache.org/docs/latest/sql-performance-tuning.html)

questions.stem: You want to count the total number of null values in a DataFrame across all columns to identify data quality issues. Which approach correctly achieves this?

options.text:
```python
from pyspark.sql.functions import col, sum as spark_sum, isnull
null_counts = df.select([spark_sum(isnull(c).cast('int')).alias(c) for c in df.columns])
null_counts.show()
```
`df.describe().filter(col('summary') == 'count').show()`
`df.printSchema()` — null columns are marked as nullable=True in the schema.

```python
df.filter(col('*').isNull()).count()
```

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct: casting `isnull(c)` to integer converts True→1, False→0, then `sum()` counts nulls per column. Iterating over `df.columns` applies this to every column in a single pass, producing a one-row summary of null counts per column.

**B** is wrong: `df.describe()` shows count (non-null count), mean, stddev, min, max for numeric and string columns, but it does not directly give null counts and omits some column types.

**C** is wrong: `printSchema()` shows whether a column allows nulls (nullable flag) but does not count actual null values in the data.

**D** is wrong: `col('*')` is not valid for `isNull()` — you cannot apply `isNull()` to all columns at once with a wildcard.

[Spark SQL, DataFrames and Datasets Guide](https://spark.apache.org/docs/latest/sql-programming-guide.html)

questions.stem: You set `spark.sql.adaptive.coalescePartitions.enabled=true` (Adaptive Query Execution). After a shuffle that produces 200 partitions, AQE detects that most partitions are very small. What does AQE do?

options.text: AQE rereads the source data with fewer initial partitions.
AQE raises a warning and stops execution until the user adjusts `spark.sql.shuffle.partitions`.
AQE spills all small partitions to disk and reprocesses them in a single task.
AQE combines small post-shuffle partitions into fewer, larger partitions at runtime, reducing task overhead.

questions.explanation:
**D** is correct: with `spark.sql.adaptive.coalescePartitions.enabled=true`, AQE inspects actual post-shuffle partition sizes and coalesces small partitions together at runtime — reducing the number of reducer tasks and cutting scheduling overhead. This is one of three main AQE features (the others being dynamic join strategy switching and skew join handling).

**A** is wrong: AQE coalescing acts on post-shuffle partitions, not on the initial read partitions.

**B** is wrong: AQE is fully automatic and does not pause execution for user input.

**C** is wrong: spilling to disk is a different mechanism for handling memory pressure, not for AQE partition coalescing.

[Spark SQL Performance Tuning](https://spark.apache.org/docs/latest/sql-performance-tuning.html)

questions.stem: Which configuration property controls the size threshold below which Spark will automatically broadcast one side of a join (without an explicit `broadcast()` hint)?

options.text: `spark.sql.shuffle.partitions`
`spark.broadcast.blockSize`
`spark.sql.autoBroadcastJoinThreshold`
`spark.executor.memoryFraction`

questions.explanation:
**C** is correct: `spark.sql.autoBroadcastJoinThreshold` (default 10 MB) sets the maximum estimated size of a DataFrame that Spark will automatically broadcast in a join. Tables whose statistics show them smaller than this threshold are broadcast without requiring a manual `broadcast()` hint. Setting it to `-1` disables auto-broadcasting.

**A** is wrong: `spark.sql.shuffle.partitions` controls the number of partitions after a shuffle, not join strategy selection.

**B** is wrong: `spark.broadcast.blockSize` controls the block size for transferring broadcast variables, not the threshold for selecting a broadcast join.

**D** is wrong: `spark.executor.memoryFraction` is not the standard config name for this; `spark.memory.fraction` controls the unified memory pool size.

[Spark SQL Performance Tuning](https://spark.apache.org/docs/latest/sql-performance-tuning.html)

questions.stem: What is **speculative execution** in Spark, and when should you enable it?

options.text: Speculative execution launches duplicate copies of slow (straggler) tasks on other executors; whichever finishes first is used and the other is killed.
Speculative execution checkpoints the DAG plan so that if the driver fails, the job restarts from the last checkpoint.
Speculative execution pre-fetches the next stage's input data while the current stage is still running.
Speculative execution compiles the physical plan to native code for faster execution on CPU.

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct: when `spark.speculation=true`, Spark monitors running tasks and, if a task is taking significantly longer than the median task time, it launches a speculative duplicate on another executor. The first copy to finish wins; the other is cancelled. This mitigates straggler tasks caused by slow hardware or network issues.

**C** is wrong: pre-fetching input is handled by the storage system (e.g., HDFS read-ahead), not speculative execution.

**B** is wrong: DAG checkpointing is handled by `df.checkpoint()`, not speculative execution.

**D** is wrong: native code generation is Tungsten's whole-stage code generation feature, not speculative execution.

[Spark Configuration](https://spark.apache.org/docs/latest/configuration.html)

questions.stem: Select TWO TRUE statements about the **External Shuffle Service** in Spark. (Select TWO)

options.text: The External Shuffle Service allows executors to be removed during dynamic allocation without losing their shuffle output files.

The External Shuffle Service is one supported way to preserve shuffle files when executors are removed during dynamic allocation, although newer Spark deployments can also use shuffle tracking instead.
The External Shuffle Service runs inside each executor JVM process.
The External Shuffle Service stores shuffle data in the driver's memory for fast retrieval.

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct: the External Shuffle Service is a long-running process on each worker node that stores and serves shuffle map output files independently of executors. When an executor is released during dynamic allocation, its shuffle files remain accessible via the service.

**B** is correct: using the External Shuffle Service is a supported way to keep shuffle data available after executor removal. Current Spark deployments can also use shuffle tracking for dynamic allocation, so the service is no longer universally required, but it remains a valid and important mechanism.

**C** is wrong: the External Shuffle Service runs as a separate process on the worker node, not inside the executor JVM.

**D** is wrong: shuffle data is stored on local disk on worker nodes, not in the driver's memory.

[Spark Configuration — Dynamic Resource Allocation](https://spark.apache.org/docs/latest/configuration.html)

questions.stem: You enable off-heap memory with `spark.memory.offHeap.enabled=true` and `spark.memory.offHeap.size=4g`. What is the primary benefit?

options.text: Off-heap memory allows Spark to store data outside the JVM heap, reducing garbage collection pressure and preventing OutOfMemoryError from JVM GC overhead.
Off-heap memory is automatically persisted to disk, providing free fault tolerance for cached DataFrames.
Off-heap memory enables the driver to directly access executor memory without network serialization.
Off-heap memory compresses all stored data using Snappy by default, reducing storage requirements.

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct: JVM garbage collection is a major source of latency in Spark jobs, especially when caching large DataFrames. By storing data in off-heap memory (outside the JVM heap, managed via `sun.misc.Unsafe` or similar APIs), Spark avoids GC pauses for that data. This is especially beneficial for long-running jobs with large caches.

**B** is wrong: off-heap memory is RAM memory outside the JVM heap — it is volatile and not persisted to disk automatically.

**C** is wrong: off-heap memory is per-executor local memory; it does not create a direct channel between driver and executor.

**D** is wrong: data in off-heap memory uses Spark's internal binary format (UnsafeRow) but is not automatically compressed.

[Spark Cluster Mode Overview](https://spark.apache.org/docs/latest/cluster-overview.html)

questions.stem: What does `df.crossJoin(other)` produce, and when is it typically used?

options.text: It returns all rows from both DataFrames, with NULLs for non-matching columns — equivalent to a full outer join.
It returns the Cartesian product of both DataFrames: every row in `df` paired with every row in `other`. Used for generating all combinations.
It returns only rows from `df` that have a matching row in `other`, similar to an inner join.
It merges the schemas of both DataFrames into a single DataFrame with all unique columns, filling missing values with null.

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct: `crossJoin()` computes the Cartesian product — if `df` has m rows and `other` has n rows, the result has m × n rows. Every row from `df` is paired with every row from `other`. Use cases include generating all combinations (e.g., pairing all products with all dates), building test datasets, or computing pairwise comparisons.

**A** is wrong: this describes a full outer join, which requires a join key.

**C** is wrong: this describes an inner join.

**D** is wrong: this describes a `unionByName()` or schema merge operation.

[Spark SQL, DataFrames and Datasets Guide](https://spark.apache.org/docs/latest/sql-programming-guide.html)

questions.stem: What is the difference between `df.sample(fraction=0.1, seed=42)` and `df.sampleBy('label', fractions={'cat': 0.3, 'dog': 0.1}, seed=42)`?

options.text: `sample()` samples with replacement by default; `sampleBy()` samples without replacement.
`sampleBy()` is deterministic and always returns the same rows; `sample()` is non-deterministic even with a seed.
Both functions produce the same result when the fraction is identical for all groups.
`sample()` draws a uniform random fraction of rows from the entire DataFrame; `sampleBy()` performs stratified sampling, allowing different sampling rates per value of a column.

questions.explanation:
**D** is correct: `sample(fraction)` applies the same sampling rate uniformly across all rows — approximately 10% of all rows. `sampleBy(col, fractions)` is stratified sampling — it samples different fractions for each stratum (value) of the specified column. In the example, 30% of 'cat' rows and 10% of 'dog' rows are sampled. This is useful for maintaining class balance in ML datasets.

**A** is wrong: `sample()` has a `withReplacement` parameter (default False) and `sampleBy()` always samples without replacement.

**B** is wrong: with the same seed, both functions are deterministic.

**C** is wrong: even if fractions match, they are fundamentally different operations — stratified vs uniform sampling.

[Spark SQL, DataFrames and Datasets Guide](https://spark.apache.org/docs/latest/sql-programming-guide.html)

questions.stem: You use `F.monotonically_increasing_id()` to add a unique ID column to a DataFrame. Which of the following is TRUE about the generated IDs?

options.text: The IDs are consecutive integers starting from 0 (0, 1, 2, 3, ...).
The IDs are guaranteed to be unique and monotonically increasing across partitions, but are NOT consecutive — there will be gaps between partition boundaries.
The IDs are random UUIDs guaranteed to be unique across distributed systems.
The IDs are the same as row indices and change if the DataFrame is repartitioned.

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct: `monotonically_increasing_id()` generates 64-bit integers that are guaranteed to be monotonically increasing and unique within the DataFrame, but they are NOT consecutive. The function encodes the partition ID in the upper bits and a row counter in the lower bits — so rows in partition 1 might have IDs 0-999, rows in partition 2 might have IDs 8589934592-8589935591. There are gaps at partition boundaries.

**A** is wrong: IDs are not consecutive — gaps exist between partitions.

**C** is wrong: IDs are not UUIDs; they are Long integers with a partition-based encoding.

**D** is wrong: while repartitioning changes the partition assignment (and thus the IDs), the IDs are not row indices in the traditional sense.

[Spark RDD Programming Guide](https://spark.apache.org/docs/latest/rdd-programming-guide.html)

questions.stem: You want to find which partition each row belongs to, for debugging purposes. Which function achieves this?

options.text: `df.withColumn('part', F.partition_id())`
`df.withColumn('part', F.spark_partition_id())`
`df.withColumn('part', F.current_partition())`
`df.withColumn('part', F.partitionOf(col('id')))`

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct: `F.spark_partition_id()` is a built-in PySpark function that returns the partition ID (as an IntegerType) for each row. It is useful for debugging partition distribution, verifying that repartitioning worked as expected, or checking for data skew across partitions.

**A** is wrong: `F.partition_id()` is not the correct function name; the correct name is `F.spark_partition_id()`.

**C** is wrong: `F.current_partition()` is not a valid function in pyspark.sql.functions.

**D** is wrong: `F.partitionOf()` is not a valid pyspark.sql.functions function.

[Spark RDD Programming Guide](https://spark.apache.org/docs/latest/rdd-programming-guide.html)

questions.stem: You have a DataFrame with a struct column `address` containing fields `street`, `city`, `state`. Which code correctly selects all fields from the struct as individual top-level columns?

options.text: `df.select(col('address.*'))`
`df.select(F.expand_struct(col('address')))`
`df.select('address.*')`
`df.select(col('address').flatten())`

questions.explanation:
**C** is correct: `df.select('address.*')` uses the `.*` notation with a string column name to expand all fields of a struct column into individual top-level columns. This is the idiomatic way to flatten one level of a StructType column.

**A** is wrong: `col('address.*')` using the Column API with `.*` does not work in PySpark — the `.*` expansion only works with string-based column references in `select()`.

**B** is wrong: `F.expand_struct()` is not a function in pyspark.sql.functions.

**D** is wrong: `.flatten()` is not a valid Column method for structs in PySpark (it exists for ArrayType in some contexts but not for struct expansion).

[Spark SQL, DataFrames and Datasets Guide](https://spark.apache.org/docs/latest/sql-programming-guide.html)

questions.stem: What does `F.percentile_approx(col('latency'), 0.95, accuracy=1000)` compute?

options.text: An approximate 95th percentile of `latency` using the Greenwald-Khanna algorithm, with `accuracy=1000` controlling the error bound.
The value at the 95th row when `latency` is sorted ascending.
The exact 95th percentile value of the `latency` column, computed with a merge-sort algorithm.
The percentage of rows where `latency` is above the 95th rank.

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct: `F.percentile_approx(col, percentage, accuracy)` computes an approximate percentile using the Greenwald-Khanna quantile summary algorithm. The `accuracy` parameter (default 10000) controls the trade-off between memory usage and result precision — higher values give more accurate results but use more memory. This is commonly used to compute medians (`0.5`) or tail latency percentiles (`0.95`, `0.99`) in large distributed datasets.

**C** is wrong: `percentile_approx` is approximate, not exact. For exact percentiles use `F.percentile()` (Spark 3.1+) or window-based approaches.

**B** is wrong: this describes a row-number-based approach, not a quantile computation.

**D** is wrong: this describes a fraction/count, not a percentile value.

[Spark SQL, DataFrames and Datasets Guide](https://spark.apache.org/docs/latest/sql-programming-guide.html)

questions.stem: You want to replace all occurrences of the string `'N/A'` with `None` (null) in a column `country`. Which code correctly achieves this?

options.text: `df.withColumn('country', F.when(col('country') == 'N/A', None).otherwise(col('country')))`
`df.na.replace('N/A', None, subset=['country'])`
`df.fillna({'country': 'N/A'})`
`df.na.fill({'country': None}).filter(col('country') != 'N/A')`

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct: `F.when(col('country') == 'N/A', None).otherwise(col('country'))` conditionally returns `None` (which becomes SQL NULL) when the value is 'N/A', and preserves the original value otherwise. This is the most explicit and readable approach.

**B** is wrong: while `df.na.replace('N/A', None, subset=['country'])` appears to work in some Spark versions, replacing with `None` via `na.replace()` is not reliably supported — the `na` methods are designed to work with non-null replacement values. The `when/otherwise` pattern in A is the idiomatic approach.

**C** is wrong: `fillna({'country': 'N/A'})` replaces null values WITH the string 'N/A' — the opposite of what we want.

**D** is wrong: `na.fill({'country': None})` is not valid — `fill()` does not accept `None` as a replacement value. Adding a filter afterward would also drop rows instead of replacing the value.

[Spark SQL Functions — PySpark documentation](https://spark.apache.org/docs/latest/api/python/reference/pyspark.sql/functions.html)

questions.stem: What does `F.date_trunc('month', col('event_ts'))` return for a timestamp `2024-03-15 14:30:00`?

options.text: The string `'2024-03'`
The integer `3` (the month number)
The date `2024-03-31` (last day of the month)

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct: `F.date_trunc(format, timestamp)` truncates a timestamp to the specified granularity. With `'month'`, it rounds the timestamp DOWN to the first moment of the month: `2024-03-01 00:00:00`. The return type is TimestampType. This is useful for grouping time-series data by month, quarter, or year without using string formatting.

**A** is wrong: `date_trunc` returns a Timestamp, not a string.

**C** is wrong: `F.month(col)` returns the month number; `date_trunc` returns a full timestamp.

**D** is wrong: `date_trunc` truncates to the START of the period, not the end.

[Spark SQL, DataFrames and Datasets Guide](https://spark.apache.org/docs/latest/sql-programming-guide.html)

questions.stem: You want to compute the last non-null value of `price` within each `ticker` partition ordered by `trade_time`. Which window function and option correctly handles this?

options.text:
```python
w = Window.partitionBy('ticker').orderBy('trade_time')
df.withColumn('last_price', F.last('price').over(w))
```

```python
w = Window.partitionBy('ticker').orderBy('trade_time').rowsBetween(Window.unboundedPreceding, Window.currentRow)
df.withColumn('last_price', F.last('price', ignorenulls=True).over(w))
```

```python
w = Window.partitionBy('ticker').orderBy('trade_time')
df.withColumn('last_price', F.last('price', skipNulls=True).over(w))
```

```python
w = Window.partitionBy('ticker')
df.withColumn('last_price', F.last('price', ignorenulls=True).over(w))
```

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct: `F.last(col, ignorenulls=True)` skips null values and returns the last non-null value seen within the window frame. The explicit `rowsBetween(unboundedPreceding, currentRow)` frame ensures we look from the start of the partition up to the current row — giving the 'last seen non-null price up to this point'. Without the explicit frame, `last()` with an `orderBy` has undefined frame behavior in some Spark versions.

**A** is wrong: without `ignorenulls=True`, `last()` returns the last value including nulls, which means a null price can overwrite a valid price.

**C** is wrong: the parameter is `ignorenulls`, not `skipNulls`.

**D** is wrong: without `orderBy`, the concept of 'last' is undefined — the partition has no ordering.

[Spark SQL, DataFrames and Datasets Guide](https://spark.apache.org/docs/latest/sql-programming-guide.html)

questions.stem: You want to divide rows within each department into 4 equal-sized buckets (quartiles) ordered by salary. Which code achieves this?

options.text:
```python
w = Window.partitionBy('dept').orderBy('salary')
df.withColumn('quartile', F.ntile(4).over(w))
```

```python
w = Window.partitionBy('dept').orderBy('salary')
df.withColumn('quartile', F.percent_rank().over(w))
```

```python
w = Window.partitionBy('dept').orderBy('salary')
df.withColumn('quartile', F.rank().over(w) / 4)
```

```python
w = Window.partitionBy('dept')
df.withColumn('quartile', F.ntile(4).over(w))
```

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct: `F.ntile(n)` divides rows within a window partition into `n` groups of roughly equal size and returns the bucket number (1 through n) for each row. `ntile(4)` assigns each salary to a quartile (Q1=1 through Q4=4) within each department.

**B** is wrong: `percent_rank()` returns a decimal between 0.0 and 1.0 (the relative rank as a fraction), not discrete bucket numbers.

**C** is wrong: `rank() / 4` divides the rank by 4, which does not create equal-sized buckets — it creates a continuous value proportional to rank.

**D** is wrong: `ntile()` requires `orderBy` in the window spec to know how to distribute rows into buckets; without ordering the bucket assignment is arbitrary.

[Spark SQL, DataFrames and Datasets Guide](https://spark.apache.org/docs/latest/sql-programming-guide.html)

questions.stem: Which of the following correctly uses `df.transform()` to apply two custom transformation functions to a DataFrame?

options.text:
```python
result = F.transform(df, add_flag).transform(drop_nulls)
```

```python
result = df.transform(add_flag, drop_nulls)
```

```python
result = df.transform([add_flag, drop_nulls])
```

questions.explanation:
**D** is correct: `df.transform(func)` applies a single function that takes a DataFrame and returns a DataFrame. To chain multiple transformations, call `.transform()` multiple times. This enables clean, readable functional-style pipelines without deeply nesting function calls.

**B** is wrong: `df.transform()` accepts only one callable argument (plus optional keyword arguments for that function); it cannot accept two positional functions.

**C** is wrong: `df.transform()` does not accept a list of functions.

**A** is wrong: `F.transform()` in pyspark.sql.functions applies a lambda to each element of an ArrayType column — it is a different function entirely and does not take a DataFrame as the first argument.

[Spark SQL, DataFrames and Datasets Guide](https://spark.apache.org/docs/latest/sql-programming-guide.html)

questions.stem: Select TWO correct statements about array functions in PySpark. (Select TWO)

options.text: `F.array_sort(col('tags'))` returns a new array with elements sorted in ascending order.
`F.array_distinct(col('tags'))` removes duplicate elements from the array, preserving order of first occurrence.
`F.array_intersect(col('a'), col('b'))` returns elements that are in array `a` but NOT in array `b`.
`F.array_sort()` raises an error if the array contains null elements.

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct: `F.array_sort(column)` sorts array elements in ascending order using natural ordering and returns a new ArrayType column. Null elements are placed at the end.

**B** is correct: `F.array_distinct(column)` removes duplicate elements from an array column and returns a new array with only distinct values, preserving the order of first occurrence.

**C** is wrong: `F.array_intersect(a, b)` returns elements present in BOTH arrays (the intersection), not the difference. For elements in `a` but not `b`, use `F.array_except(a, b)`.

**D** is wrong: `F.array_sort()` handles null elements by placing them at the end of the sorted array; it does not raise an error.

[Spark SQL, DataFrames and Datasets Guide](https://spark.apache.org/docs/latest/sql-programming-guide.html)

questions.stem: You have a DataFrame with a `MapType(StringType(), IntegerType())` column named `scores`. You want to extract just the keys of the map as an array. Which expression is correct?

options.text: `df.withColumn('keys', col('scores').keys())`
`df.withColumn('keys', F.keys(col('scores')))`
`df.withColumn('keys', F.map_keys(col('scores')))`
`df.withColumn('keys', F.map_entries(col('scores')).getField('key'))`

questions.explanation:
**C** is correct: `F.map_keys(column)` is the built-in PySpark function that extracts the keys of a MapType column as an ArrayType column. Similarly, `F.map_values(column)` extracts values, and `F.map_entries(column)` returns an array of structs with `key` and `value` fields.

**A** is wrong: PySpark Column objects do not have a `.keys()` method; this is a Python dictionary method.

**B** is wrong: `F.keys()` is not a function in pyspark.sql.functions; the correct function is `F.map_keys()`.

**D** is wrong: `F.map_entries()` returns an array of key-value structs, not just keys — and `.getField('key')` on that array would require further exploding.

[Spark SQL, DataFrames and Datasets Guide](https://spark.apache.org/docs/latest/sql-programming-guide.html)

questions.stem: You want to serialize several columns into a single JSON string column. Which code correctly converts `name`, `age`, and `city` into a JSON string column called `payload`?

options.text: `df.withColumn('payload', F.struct('name', 'age', 'city').toJSON())`
`df.withColumn('payload', F.json_string(col('name'), col('age'), col('city')))`
`df.withColumn('payload', F.to_json('name', 'age', 'city'))`
`df.withColumn('payload', F.to_json(F.struct('name', 'age', 'city')))`

questions.explanation:
**D** is correct: `F.to_json(F.struct(*cols))` first combines the specified columns into a StructType column using `F.struct()`, then serializes the struct to a JSON string. The result is a StringType column containing a JSON object like `{"name":"Alice","age":30,"city":"NYC"}`.

**B** is wrong: `F.json_string()` is not a function in pyspark.sql.functions.

**C** is wrong: `F.to_json()` accepts a single Column argument (typically a struct or map column), not multiple string column names as positional arguments.

**A** is wrong: PySpark Column objects do not have a `.toJSON()` method.

[Spark SQL, DataFrames and Datasets Guide](https://spark.apache.org/docs/latest/sql-programming-guide.html)

questions.stem: You have a StringType column `raw_json` containing JSON strings like `'{"user":"alice","score":95}'`. You want to parse it into a struct with fields `user` (string) and `score` (integer). Which code is correct?

options.text: `df.withColumn('parsed', F.parse_json(col('raw_json')))`
`df.withColumn('parsed', F.json_tuple(col('raw_json'), 'user', 'score'))`

```python
from pyspark.sql.types import StructType, StructField, StringType, IntegerType
schema = StructType([StructField('user', StringType()), StructField('score', IntegerType())])
df.withColumn('parsed', F.from_json(col('raw_json'), schema))
```
`df.withColumn('parsed', F.from_json(col('raw_json')))`

questions.explanation:
**C** is correct: `F.from_json(column, schema)` parses a JSON string column into a StructType column using the provided schema. The schema can be a `StructType` object or a DDL-format string like `'user STRING, score INT'`. The result is a struct column with `parsed.user` and `parsed.score` accessible via dot notation or `getField()`.

**A** is wrong: `F.parse_json()` is not a function in pyspark.sql.functions; the correct function is `F.from_json()`.

**B** is wrong: `F.json_tuple(json_col, *fields)` extracts specified fields from a JSON string into individual string columns (not a struct), and all extracted values are StringType — so `score` would not be an integer.

**D** is wrong: `F.from_json()` requires a schema argument; without it, Spark cannot determine the output structure and raises an error.

[Spark SQL, DataFrames and Datasets Guide](https://spark.apache.org/docs/latest/sql-programming-guide.html)

questions.stem: Which code correctly writes a DataFrame to Parquet with Snappy compression and validates the write options used?

options.text: `df.write.parquet('/output', compression='snappy')`
`df.write.option('compression', 'snappy').parquet('/output')`
`df.write.format('parquet').option('codec', 'snappy').save('/output')`
`df.write.format('parquet').option('spark.sql.parquet.compression.codec', 'snappy').save('/output')`

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct: `df.write.option('compression', 'snappy').parquet(path)` sets the Parquet compression codec to Snappy via the DataFrameWriter's option API. Valid values include `'snappy'`, `'gzip'`, `'lz4'`, `'brotli'`, `'zstd'`, and `'none'`.

**A** is wrong: `df.write.parquet(path)` does not accept a `compression` keyword argument directly — compression must be set via `.option()` on the DataFrameWriter.

**C** is wrong: for Parquet, the DataFrameWriter option key is `'compression'`, not `'codec'`. Using `'codec'` would be silently ignored in most Spark versions.

**D** is wrong: `spark.sql.parquet.compression.codec` is the Spark configuration property (set via `spark.conf.set()`), not the DataFrameWriter option key. The DataFrameWriter option key is simply `'compression'`.

[Spark SQL Data Sources — Parquet](https://spark.apache.org/docs/latest/sql-data-sources-parquet.html)

questions.stem: You need to read a JSON file where each line is a valid JSON object, but you want to enforce a known schema instead of inferring it. Which code is correct?

options.text: `spark.read.json('/path/data.json')`

```python
schema = 'id LONG, name STRING, amount DOUBLE'
spark.read.schema(schema).json('/path/data.json')
```
`spark.read.format('json').option('enforceSchema', True).load('/path/data.json')`
`spark.read.json('/path/data.json', inferSchema=False)`

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct: `spark.read.schema(schema).json(path)` applies a user-defined schema (as a DDL string or StructType) when reading JSON, bypassing schema inference entirely. This improves read performance (no sampling pass), ensures correct types, and avoids schema mismatches from dirty data.

**A** is wrong: without `.schema()`, Spark infers the schema by sampling the data — which is slower and may not produce the types you expect.

**C** is wrong: `option('enforceSchema', True)` is a valid option that enforces the provided schema against JSON fields, but it still requires a schema to be provided via `.schema()` — the option alone without a schema does not help.

**D** is wrong: `inferSchema=False` is not a valid JSON reader option in PySpark (it exists for CSV readers); for JSON, you supply a schema via `.schema()`.

[Spark SQL, DataFrames and Datasets Guide](https://spark.apache.org/docs/latest/sql-programming-guide.html)

questions.stem: You use `F.regexp_extract(col('log_line'), r'(\d{3})', 1)` on the string `'ERROR 404 Not Found'`. What does the `1` argument specify and what is the output?

options.text: The `1` specifies the minimum match length; the output is `'4'` (the first digit).
The `1` specifies the capture group index; the output is `'404'` (the first group of 3 consecutive digits).
The `1` specifies the occurrence number; the output is the first match of any digit.
The `1` specifies that only 1 character should be extracted; the output is `'4'`.

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct: `F.regexp_extract(column, pattern, idx)` extracts the content of a specific capture group from the first regex match. The third argument `idx` is the capture group index — `0` returns the entire match, `1` returns the first capture group, `2` returns the second, etc. The pattern `(\d{3})` has one capture group matching exactly 3 consecutive digits. In `'ERROR 404 Not Found'`, the first match of 3 digits is `'404'`, so the output is `'404'`.

**A** is wrong: the third argument is a group index, not a minimum match length.

**C** is wrong: `regexp_extract` always returns the first match; the third argument selects the capture group within that match, not which occurrence.

**D** is wrong: the argument controls group selection, not character count.

[Spark SQL, DataFrames and Datasets Guide](https://spark.apache.org/docs/latest/sql-programming-guide.html)

questions.stem: Select TWO correct statements about chained `F.when().when().otherwise()` expressions in PySpark. (Select TWO)

options.text: Conditions are evaluated in order from top to bottom; once a condition matches, subsequent conditions are not checked for that row.
If no condition matches and `.otherwise()` is omitted, the result is `null` for that row.
The `.otherwise()` clause must always be present; omitting it raises an AnalysisException.
All conditions are evaluated independently and the result is the logical OR of all matching values.

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct: `F.when(cond1, val1).when(cond2, val2).otherwise(default)` is equivalent to SQL CASE WHEN THEN ... ELSE END. Conditions are checked in order — the first matching condition determines the output, and subsequent conditions are short-circuited for that row.

**B** is correct: if no condition in the chain matches and `.otherwise()` is not provided, PySpark returns `null` (SQL NULL) for that row — there is no error, just a null result.

**C** is wrong: `.otherwise()` is optional; omitting it is valid and results in nulls for unmatched rows.

**D** is wrong: conditions are not evaluated as a logical OR — only the first matching condition's value is returned; subsequent conditions are not evaluated.

[Spark SQL, DataFrames and Datasets Guide](https://spark.apache.org/docs/latest/sql-programming-guide.html)

questions.stem: You want to create a Delta table registered in the metastore that points to data stored at `/mnt/delta/sales` (an external location). Which SQL statement is correct?

options.text: `CREATE TABLE sales USING DELTA LOCATION '/mnt/delta/sales'`
`CREATE MANAGED TABLE sales USING DELTA AT '/mnt/delta/sales'`
`CREATE DELTA TABLE sales PATH '/mnt/delta/sales'`
`CREATE TABLE sales AS SELECT * FROM delta.\`/mnt/delta/sales\``

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct: `CREATE TABLE table_name USING DELTA LOCATION 'path'` creates an external Delta table — the table metadata is registered in the metastore, but the data at the specified path is not owned by the metastore (dropping the table does not delete the data). This is the standard SQL syntax for registering an existing Delta table at a path.

**B** is wrong: `CREATE MANAGED TABLE` is not valid Spark SQL syntax; managed vs external is determined by the presence of `LOCATION`, not a keyword.

**C** is wrong: `CREATE DELTA TABLE ... PATH ...` is not valid SQL syntax.

**D** is wrong: `CREATE TABLE ... AS SELECT ...` (CTAS) creates a NEW table by executing a query and writing the results — it would copy data, not register an existing path as an external table.

[Spark SQL Data Sources — Delta Lake](https://spark.apache.org/docs/latest/sql-data-sources.html)

questions.stem: You have a Hive-partitioned table `events` with partition directories like `year=2023/month=01/`. The table was created externally and new partition directories were added to the filesystem. Which SQL command synchronizes the metastore to recognize the new partitions?

options.text: `REFRESH TABLE events`
`MSCK REPAIR TABLE events`
`ALTER TABLE events RECOVER PARTITIONS`
`SYNC TABLE events`

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct: `MSCK REPAIR TABLE table_name` (Metastore Check) scans the table's storage path for new partition directories (in Hive-style `key=value` format) and adds any discovered partitions to the metastore catalog. This is necessary when partitions are added directly to the filesystem without using `ALTER TABLE ADD PARTITION`.

**A** is wrong: `REFRESH TABLE` refreshes the cache and schema metadata for a table but does not discover new filesystem partitions for Hive-style tables.

**C** is wrong: `ALTER TABLE ... RECOVER PARTITIONS` is a Databricks-specific alias for MSCK REPAIR TABLE on Delta/some formats, but `MSCK REPAIR TABLE` is the standard SQL command.

**D** is wrong: `SYNC TABLE` is not a valid Spark SQL command.

[Spark SQL Data Sources](https://spark.apache.org/docs/latest/sql-data-sources.html)

questions.stem: You execute `CACHE TABLE monthly_sales` in Spark SQL. What happens, and how do you remove the table from the cache?

options.text: `CACHE TABLE` writes the table to a persistent cache on disk; use `DELETE CACHE monthly_sales` to remove it.
`CACHE TABLE` eagerly materializes and caches the table's data in memory for the current session; use `UNCACHE TABLE monthly_sales` to remove it.
`CACHE TABLE` is equivalent to `df.cache()` — it marks the table for lazy caching on next use; use `DROP TABLE` to clear it.
`CACHE TABLE` makes the table available for use by other SparkSessions; use `RELEASE TABLE monthly_sales` to stop sharing it.

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct: `CACHE TABLE table_name` in Spark SQL eagerly caches the table's data in memory (unlike `df.cache()` which is lazy). After execution, the data is immediately stored in the Spark in-memory store. `UNCACHE TABLE table_name` removes it from the cache. You can also use `REFRESH TABLE` to invalidate the cache if the underlying data changes.

**A** is wrong: `CACHE TABLE` stores data in memory (RAM), not on persistent disk.

**C** is wrong: SQL `CACHE TABLE` is EAGER (unlike `df.cache()` which is lazy) — it triggers computation immediately.

**D** is wrong: cached tables are session-scoped (or cluster-scoped depending on configuration) but are not shared across sessions as a persistent object.

[Spark SQL, DataFrames and Datasets Guide](https://spark.apache.org/docs/latest/sql-programming-guide.html)

questions.stem: You want to add two new columns to an existing table `orders` using SQL: `discount_pct DOUBLE` and `notes STRING`. Which SQL statement is correct?

options.text: `ALTER TABLE orders ADD COLUMN discount_pct DOUBLE, notes STRING`
`ALTER TABLE orders ADD COLUMNS (discount_pct DOUBLE, notes STRING)`
`UPDATE TABLE orders ADD COLUMNS (discount_pct DOUBLE, notes STRING)`
`MODIFY TABLE orders ADD (discount_pct DOUBLE, notes STRING)`

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct: the standard Spark SQL syntax for adding multiple columns is `ALTER TABLE table_name ADD COLUMNS (col1 TYPE1, col2 TYPE2, ...)`. The `COLUMNS` keyword (plural) with parentheses allows specifying one or more columns in a single statement.

**A** is wrong: `ADD COLUMN` (singular, without parentheses) is valid for adding a single column in some SQL dialects, but for multiple columns in Spark SQL the correct syntax is `ADD COLUMNS (...)` with parentheses.

**C** is wrong: `UPDATE TABLE` is not valid DDL syntax; `UPDATE` is a DML statement for modifying row values, not table structure.

**D** is wrong: `MODIFY TABLE` is not valid Spark SQL syntax.

[Spark SQL, DataFrames and Datasets Guide](https://spark.apache.org/docs/latest/sql-programming-guide.html)

questions.stem: You want to override `spark.sql.shuffle.partitions` for only a specific SQL query, without affecting the rest of the session. Which approach is correct?

options.text: Use `SET spark.sql.shuffle.partitions = 50` before the query; this automatically reverts after the query completes.

```python
spark.conf.set('spark.sql.shuffle.partitions', '50')
result = spark.sql('SELECT ...')
spark.conf.set('spark.sql.shuffle.partitions', '200')
```
Use a SQL hint inside the query: `SELECT /*+ SHUFFLE_PARTITIONS(50) */ ...` — this is supported in Spark 3.x+
Create a new SparkSession with `spark.newSession()` and set the config there; the original session is not affected.

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct: temporarily setting `spark.sql.shuffle.partitions` to 50, running the query, then restoring the original value is the standard programmatic approach to override a config for a single query without permanently changing the session setting. This gives you precise control over which query gets the override.

**A** is wrong: `SET spark.sql.shuffle.partitions = 50` in Spark SQL modifies the session configuration and it persists for the remainder of the session — it does NOT automatically revert after one query.

**C** is wrong: `SHUFFLE_PARTITIONS` is not a supported SQL hint in PySpark. The `REPARTITION(n)` hint controls the output partition count of a query but is NOT equivalent to `spark.sql.shuffle.partitions` for shuffle operations within the query (e.g., for join and aggregation exchanges).

**D** is wrong: while `spark.newSession()` does create a child session that inherits parent config and can override it independently, this is a heavyweight approach that requires rewriting the query to use the new session object — it is not a standard pattern for single-query overrides.

[Spark Configuration — Performance Tuning](https://spark.apache.org/docs/latest/sql-performance-tuning.html)

questions.stem: You run `df.explain(True)` and see both `== Analyzed Logical Plan ==` and `== Physical Plan ==` sections. A filter `age > 18` appears in the Analyzed Logical Plan but in the Physical Plan it appears BEFORE the file scan. What optimization does this indicate?

options.text: Column pruning — Spark removed columns not needed by the filter.
Predicate pushdown — Catalyst moved the filter closer to the data source so fewer rows are read from disk.
Broadcast join — Spark detected that `age` is small enough to broadcast.
AQE coalescing — Spark merged partitions after reading to reduce task count.

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct: predicate pushdown is a Catalyst optimization where filter conditions are pushed as close to the data source as possible. When a filter appears ABOVE the scan in the logical plan but BELOW (or inside) the scan in the physical plan (e.g., `PushedFilters` inside a `FileScan`), it means Catalyst pushed the predicate into the file reader itself. For Parquet and ORC files, this allows the reader to skip entire row groups without reading them, dramatically reducing I/O.

**A** is wrong: column pruning removes unneeded columns from the scan (you'd see fewer columns in the physical FileScan than in the original schema), not filter reordering.

**C** is wrong: broadcast join optimization appears as `BroadcastHashJoin` in the physical plan and involves join strategies, not filter placement.

**D** is wrong: AQE coalescing happens at runtime after shuffle execution, not during the static planning phase shown by `explain()`.

[Spark SQL Performance Tuning](https://spark.apache.org/docs/latest/sql-performance-tuning.html)

questions.stem: What is the key difference between `df.checkpoint()` and `df.localCheckpoint()`?

options.text: `df.checkpoint()` truncates the lineage and saves data to a reliable distributed store (e.g., HDFS); `df.localCheckpoint()` truncates the lineage and saves data to each executor's local disk — faster but not fault-tolerant.
`df.checkpoint()` is lazy and only materializes when an action is called; `df.localCheckpoint()` is eager and materializes immediately.
`df.localCheckpoint()` saves data to the driver's local disk; `df.checkpoint()` saves to executors.
Both checkpoint methods are identical except that `localCheckpoint()` uses Snappy compression while `checkpoint()` uses GZip.

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct: `df.checkpoint()` (reliable checkpoint) truncates the logical lineage and persists data to the checkpoint directory set via `spark.sparkContext.setCheckpointDir()` — typically HDFS or cloud storage. This survives executor failures. `df.localCheckpoint()` also truncates lineage but stores data on each executor's local disk — it is faster (no network transfer to shared storage) but the data is lost if the executor dies, so it is NOT fault-tolerant. Both are used to break long lineage chains that would cause stack overflow or slow recomputation.

**B** is wrong: both checkpoint methods trigger computation eagerly (they are actions that materialize the DataFrame).

**C** is wrong: `localCheckpoint()` saves to executor LOCAL disk, not the driver's disk.

**D** is wrong: checkpoint methods do not specify or differ by compression codec.

[Spark RDD Programming Guide — Checkpointing](https://spark.apache.org/docs/latest/rdd-programming-guide.html)

questions.stem: Select TWO correct statements about Spark `StorageLevel` options when persisting a DataFrame. (Select TWO)

options.text: `MEMORY_ONLY` stores deserialized Java objects in JVM heap memory; if a partition doesn't fit, it is NOT stored and must be recomputed on each access.
`MEMORY_AND_DISK` stores partitions in memory if they fit; partitions that don't fit are written to disk, from which they are read on next access instead of being recomputed.
`DISK_ONLY` stores data in memory using a compressed serialized format, falling back to disk only on OOM.
`MEMORY_AND_DISK` is equivalent to `MEMORY_ONLY` when the dataset fits entirely in memory.

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct: `StorageLevel.MEMORY_ONLY` (the level used by `df.cache()`) stores partitions as deserialized Java/Scala objects in JVM heap. If a partition doesn't fit in the available storage memory, Spark does NOT store it — it will be recomputed from scratch when needed. This is the fastest level for access but can cause recomputation overhead for large datasets.

**B** is correct: `MEMORY_AND_DISK` stores partitions in memory where possible, and spills excess partitions to local disk. On next access, disk-spilled partitions are read from disk (slower than memory but faster than full recomputation).

**C** is wrong: this describes `MEMORY_ONLY_SER` (serialized in-memory) or `DISK_ONLY_SER` behavior — `DISK_ONLY` stores ALL data on disk regardless of available memory.

**D** is wrong: while it is true that `MEMORY_AND_DISK` and `MEMORY_ONLY` produce identical results when all data fits in memory, they are NOT equivalent in their behavior definition or fallback strategy — `MEMORY_AND_DISK` spills to disk when partitions don't fit, while `MEMORY_ONLY` recomputes them.

[Spark RDD Programming Guide — Storage Levels](https://spark.apache.org/docs/latest/rdd-programming-guide.html)
